options.text: Because `{}` is inferred too vaguely, so indexed writes need a defined shape.
options.feedback: Correct: empty object inference lacks index/value info, so property updates are unsafe.

options.text: Because `reduce` never supports generic inference under any circumstances.
options.feedback: Reduce can infer types in many cases, especially with precise initial values.

options.text: Because callbacks passed to `reduce` must always return `Promise` values.
options.feedback: Reduce callbacks can be synchronous; async return is optional and unrelated here.

options.text: Because object literals cannot be used as `reduce` initial values in TypeScript.
options.feedback: Object initial values are valid; the issue is missing explicit structural typing.

questions.question: Why does `reduce` with an initial value of `{}` often need explicit accumulator typing in TypeScript?

whyOptions.text: Because the compiler cannot guarantee key existence or value type from bare `{}`.
whyOptions.feedback: Exactly: without an index signature, dynamic property access remains type-unsafe.

whyOptions.text: Because TypeScript disables bracket notation unless `any` is globally enabled.
whyOptions.feedback: Bracket notation is allowed; safety checks fail when structure is undefined.

whyOptions.text: Because generic `U` only works with primitive accumulator types.
whyOptions.feedback: Generic accumulators can be objects; they just need a concrete declared shape.

whyOptions.text: Because `reduce` always narrows accumulator types to `never` during iteration.
whyOptions.feedback: TypeScript does not force `never` here; constraints come from initial inference.

options.text: Pipe, because it transforms and validates request body authorization rules.

whyOptions.text: Because interceptors cannot access request objects in NestJS.
whyOptions.feedback: Interceptors can access context, but authorization remains a guard concern.

whyOptions.text: Because pipes are disabled when `transform: true` is enabled.
whyOptions.feedback: Transform option belongs to validation pipe behavior and does not disable pipes.

questions.question: Why does the CMS note store content definitions as JSON schema/config objects?

whyOptions.text: Definitions store field blueprint; entries store item data referencing that blueprint.
whyOptions.feedback: Exactly: one defines structure, the other stores actual records and values.

whyOptions.text: Definitions store published posts, while entries only store tenant membership roles.
whyOptions.feedback: Membership is separate; entries hold content items tied to definitions.

whyOptions.text: Definitions and entries are duplicates kept for backup and rollback only.
whyOptions.feedback: They represent distinct responsibilities, not redundant backup copies.

whyOptions.text: Definitions are static SQL views, while entries are temporary cache rows.
whyOptions.feedback: The note describes JSON-backed domain entities, not view-cache architecture.
